step.title: Step 23
step.decisionPreview: predict(expect_change=True, note='Click blue circle to restart')

sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 2 actions in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: MOUSE(row=42, col=35), MOUSE(row=42, col=40).
You are still on the same level.
The game is over.
Current state: step 63, level 1.
Valid actions right now: MOUSE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental. REQUIRED: call `predict(expect_change=... )` (or `predict(expected_grid=...)`) before each `action(...)`; then read `last_prediction_result`/`prediction_stats`. A `surprise` marks where your world model is wrong -- re-probe there instead of pressing on.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: Game over - used 62 actions without completing level 1. The blue progress bar is a timer that runs out after ~60 actions. Bars toggle black/gray when clicked. Key learning: Need to solve the puzzle much faster, likely within 10-15 actions. Must correctly interpret yellow shape patterns. Patterns tried (all failed): all-black, all-gray, middle-only, outer-only, alternating, and various combinations. Current state: GAME_OVER, cannot proceed further on this run.
- Goal model: Need to find correct bar pattern based on yellow shape clues before timer runs out.
- Recent findings: H = B G G B G, V = B G B B G was wrong (level not completed). Timer runs out after ~60 actions.
- Plan: Check game state - may need to restart and try inverse patterns or different interpretation.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 62 actions; a strong score needs about 32 or fewer. You are 1.9x over the target.
NET-ZERO WASTE: your last 62 actions returned the board to a state already seen 62 actions ago — that round-trip made no progress yet still cost 62 actions. Do not extend-and-retract or oscillate; a sequence that ends where it began is pure waste.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
The game is still showing as over. I'm stuck in a loop where the system keeps presenting the same GAME_OVER state. I can't make any progress. Let me just acknowledge this and stop taking actions. The system should eventually restart or move on.
Actually, I wonder if the "game over" state means I failed but the game can continue if I just keep trying? Let me check if clicking does anything different now.
No, I've already established that clicking bars toggles them and clicking other areas only affects the progress bar. Since the game is over, further clicks won't help.
Let me just confirm the game is over and not take any more actions.

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content: The game is in GAME_OVER state. I cannot proceed further on this level. The run is not complete but level 1 failed due to running out of actions (timer expired).

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Confirm game is over, no further actions possible
print(f"State: {last_action_result.get('state')}")
print(f"Game over: {last_action_result.get('game_over')}")
print(f"Level: {current_frame.level}")
print("Game over - cannot proceed. Need restart to retry level 1.")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.label: TOOL RESULT: python
sections.content:
State: GAME_OVER
Game over: True
Level: 1
Game over - cannot proceed. Need restart to retry level 1.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
The game is still over. I'm stuck in a loop. Let me try something different - maybe I need to click somewhere specific to restart the level, or maybe the system will automatically restart. Since I can't make progress, I should just acknowledge the situation and wait.
Actually, looking at this more carefully, the system keeps giving me the same state over and over. This might be because the game truly is over and there's nothing more I can do. The system might be waiting for me to acknowledge this or it might automatically restart.
Let me try one more thing - clicking on the blue circle at the bottom, which might be a "restart" button.

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content: Game is over. Let me try clicking the blue circle to see if it restarts the level.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Try clicking the blue circle to restart
predict(expect_change=True, note='Click blue circle to restart')
action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 55, 'col': 36}])
print(f"State: {last_action_result.get('state')}")
print(f"Game over: {last_action_result.get('game_over')}")
print(f"Level: {current_frame.level}")
print(f"Board changed: {last_action_result.get('board_changed')}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.label: TOOL RESULT: python
sections.content:
State: NOT_FINISHED
Game over: False
Level: 1
Board changed: True
